news_summary: Advancements in AI are uncovering hidden ocean currents, Germany is transforming former coal mines into thriving lakes, bee populations are flourishing in unexpected places, and Caribbean nations are celebrating their unique bird species.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès de l'intelligence artificielle permettent de mettre au jour des courants océaniques cachés, l'Allemagne transforme d'anciennes mines de charbon en lacs florissants, les populations d'abeilles prospèrent dans des endroits inattendus, et les pays des Caraïbes célèbrent leurs espèces d'oiseaux uniques.
news_summary_es: Los avances en inteligencia artificial están revelando corrientes oceánicas ocultas, Alemania está transformando antiguas minas de carbón en lagos florecientes, las poblaciones de abejas están prosperando en lugares inesperados y los países del Caribe están celebrando sus especies de aves únicas.

articles.title: AI just revealed ocean currents we’ve never been able to see
articles.summary: A new AI-driven method called GOFLOW is turning weather satellite images into highly detailed maps of ocean currents. By tracking how temperature patterns shift over time, it can reveal fast-moving, small-scale currents that were previously impossible to observe directly. These currents are key to understanding climate, marine ecosystems, and carbon storage. The breakthrough works using satellites already in orbit, making it both powerful and cost-effective.
articles.content:
Scientists have introduced a new way to track ocean surface currents across vast areas with far greater detail than before. The technique, known as GOFLOW (Geostationary Ocean Flow), uses deep learning to analyze thermal images captured by weather satellites that are already in orbit. Because it relies on existing satellites, the method delivers a major advance in ocean monitoring without requiring new equipment in space.

The research was led by Luc Lenain of UC San Diego's Scripps Institution of Oceanography and Kaushik Srinivasan, a Scripps alumnus now at UCLA. Their findings were published in Nature Geoscience. Co-authors Roy Barkan of Tel Aviv University and Nick Pizzo of the University of Rhode Island also trained at Scripps. Funding came from the Office of Naval Research, NASA and the European Research Council.

Why Ocean Currents Matter for Climate and Life

Ocean currents are essential to how the planet works. They move heat around the globe, transfer carbon between the atmosphere and the deep ocean, and circulate nutrients that support marine ecosystems. They also play a critical role in real-world situations such as search and rescue efforts and tracking oil spills.

Despite their importance, accurately measuring currents across large regions has been difficult. Some satellites estimate currents indirectly by observing changes in sea surface height, but they usually revisit the same area only about once every 10 days -- far too slow to capture currents that can form and fade within hours. Ships and coastal radar can detect rapid changes, but only over limited areas.

The Missing Link in Ocean Mixing

This limitation has left scientists with a major blind spot at the scales where vertical mixing occurs. Vertical mixing happens when surface waters move downward or deeper waters rise, and it is driven by features that can be smaller than 10 kilometers (six miles) and change quickly.

Understanding this process is crucial. It brings nutrients from the deep ocean up to the surface, supporting marine life, and carries carbon dioxide downward where it can be stored long-term. Without detailed observations, much of this activity has remained difficult to measure directly.

Turning Satellite Images Into Ocean Flow Maps

The idea for GOFLOW began in 2023 when Lenain examined thermal images of the North Atlantic from the GOES-East satellite, which is typically used for weather monitoring. These images are captured as often as every five minutes and show clouds as well as patterns of warm and cool water moving across the ocean surface.

Lenain noticed that major currents such as the Gulf Stream were visible in these temperature patterns. This observation led to the idea of converting those patterns into a new way to measure ocean currents.

How AI Tracks Ocean Currents

To make this possible, the research team trained a neural network to recognize how temperature patterns on the ocean surface shift and change shape under the influence of currents. The system learned from detailed computer simulations of ocean circulation, which linked specific temperature patterns to known water velocities.

Once trained, the model analyzed sequences of satellite images and tracked how these patterns moved over time. From this motion, it could determine the underlying currents responsible for the changes.

"Weather satellites have been observing the ocean surface for years," said Lenain. "The breakthrough was learning how to turn that time-lapse into hourly maps of currents by tracking how temperature patterns bend, stretch and move from one hour to the next."

Testing Accuracy Against Real-World Data

The researchers evaluated GOFLOW by comparing its results to direct measurements collected by ships in the Gulf Stream region during 2023, as well as traditional satellite methods based on ocean topography. The results closely matched both sources.

However, GOFLOW provided much sharper detail, especially for small, fast-moving features such as eddies and boundary layers. Earlier methods often smoothed these features into broad averages. With the improved resolution, the team was able to detect key statistical patterns of small, intense currents that drive vertical mixing. Until now, these patterns had mainly been seen in simulations rather than direct observations.

"This opens a range of exciting possibilities in physical oceanography that, until now, were largely accessible only through simulations," said Lenain. "Using GOFLOW, we can now measure key signatures of these small, intense currents using real observations rather than relying almost entirely on simulations. This opens the door to testing long-standing ideas about how the ocean takes up heat and carbon."

No New Satellites Required

Because GOFLOW works with data from existing geostationary satellites, it does not require launching new instruments into space. Over time, the method could be integrated into weather forecasting systems and climate models. By capturing rapidly changing currents, it may improve predictions related to air-sea interactions, marine debris movement and ecosystem dynamics.

Challenges and Future Expansion

Cloud cover remains a limitation, since clouds block the thermal images that GOFLOW depends on. The research team plans to combine additional satellite data sources to fill in these gaps and achieve more consistent coverage.

Work is already underway to expand the method to a global scale. The team has also made its data products and code publicly available, which could help other scientists build on the approach and explore new applications.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific breakthrough using AI to map ocean currents in unprecedented detail, which has broad implications for understanding climate, marine ecosystems, and carbon storage. This method leverages existing satellite data, making it cost-effective and scalable, with potential to improve climate models and environmental monitoring globally. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a tangible advancement with large-scale environmental benefits.
articles.personality_title: AI reveals detailed ocean currents using weather satellites
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have struggled to see small, fast ocean currents clearly because current satellite methods update too slowly and ships cover only small areas. These currents are important for moving heat, nutrients, and carbon in the ocean.

**What happened** – A new AI method called GOFLOW was created by researchers from UC San Diego and UCLA. It uses deep learning to study temperature patterns in images taken every five minutes by weather satellites already in space. This method tracks how these patterns move to map ocean currents in much finer detail than before.

**Impact** – GOFLOW can now show small, quick currents that were hard to detect until now. These currents help mix ocean layers, supporting marine life and storing carbon. Using existing satellites means this method is cheaper and easier to use worldwide. It could improve weather and climate models by giving better information about ocean behavior.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to expand GOFLOW to cover oceans globally and combine it with other satellite data to work even when clouds block the view. They have shared their data and code so other scientists can build on their work.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new AI technique transforms weather satellite images into detailed maps of ocean currents, offering better insight into ocean processes without needing new satellites.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'IA révèle en détail les courants océaniques grâce aux satellites météo
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques peinent à observer clairement les petits courants océaniques rapides, car les satellites actuels mettent trop de temps à actualiser les données et les navires ne couvrent que de petites zones. Ces courants sont importants pour le déplacement de la chaleur, des nutriments et du carbone dans l'océan.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une nouvelle méthode d'IA appelée GOFLOW a été créée par des chercheurs de l'UC San Diego et de UCLA. Elle utilise l'apprentissage profond pour analyser les motifs de température dans des images prises toutes les cinq minutes par des satellites météorologiques déjà en orbite. Cette méthode suit le déplacement de ces motifs pour cartographier les courants océaniques avec beaucoup plus de précision.

**Impact** – GOFLOW peut désormais montrer de petits courants rapides difficiles à détecter auparavant. Ces courants aident à mélanger les couches océaniques, soutenant la vie marine et le stockage du carbone. Utiliser les satellites existants rend cette méthode moins coûteuse et plus facile à déployer dans le monde entier. Elle pourrait améliorer les modèles météo et climat grâce à une meilleure connaissance des océans.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d'étendre GOFLOW à l'échelle mondiale et de le combiner avec d'autres données satellitaires pour fonctionner même quand les nuages bloquent la vue. Ils ont partagé leurs données et codes pour que d'autres scientifiques puissent poursuivre ces travaux.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle technique d'IA transforme les images satellites météo en cartes détaillées des courants océaniques, offrant une meilleure compréhension des processus océaniques sans lancer de nouveaux satellites.
articles.personality_title_es: La IA revela corrientes oceánicas detalladas usando satélites meteorológicos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han tenido dificultades para ver claramente las corrientes oceánicas pequeñas y rápidas porque los satélites actuales actualizan datos muy lentamente y los barcos solo cubren áreas pequeñas. Estas corrientes son importantes para mover calor, nutrientes y carbono en el océano.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo método de IA llamado GOFLOW fue creado por investigadores de UC San Diego y UCLA. Utiliza aprendizaje profundo para estudiar patrones de temperatura en imágenes tomadas cada cinco minutos por satélites meteorológicos ya en el espacio. Este método rastrea cómo se mueven estos patrones para mapear las corrientes oceánicas con mucho más detalle.

**Impacto** – GOFLOW ahora puede mostrar corrientes pequeñas y rápidas que antes eran difíciles de detectar. Estas corrientes ayudan a mezclar las capas del océano, apoyando la vida marina y almacenando carbono. Usar satélites existentes hace que este método sea más barato y fácil de usar en todo el mundo. Podría mejorar los modelos del clima y el tiempo al dar mejor información sobre el comportamiento del océano.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean expandir GOFLOW para cubrir los océanos a nivel global y combinarlo con otros datos satelitales para que funcione incluso cuando las nubes bloquean la vista. Han compartido sus datos y código para que otros científicos puedan continuar el trabajo.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Una nueva técnica de IA convierte imágenes de satélites meteorológicos en mapas detallados de corrientes oceánicas, ofreciendo mejor comprensión de los procesos oceánicos sin necesidad de nuevos satélites.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vast ocean surface seen from above, with swirling, colorful thermal patterns in soft blues, greens, and warm oranges gently flowing and intertwining like ribbons, symbolizing dynamic ocean currents; in the background, a subtle silhouette of a geostationary satellite orbiting Earth, connecting the natural ocean scene with advanced AI technology tracking the invisible movements beneath the waves.

articles.title: Germany’s coal mines are now Europe’s largest lake district
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM When the last miners left the open-cast lignite pits of eastern Germany’s Lusatia region, they left behind craters stretching more than 200 feet (60 meters) deep. What followed was not restoration in any conventional sense. It was construction: the deliberate, painstaking work of building a landscape that had never […]
The post Germany’s coal mines are now Europe’s largest lake district first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

When the last miners left the open-cast lignite pits of eastern Germany’s Lusatia region, they left behind craters stretching more than 200 feet (60 meters) deep. What followed was not restoration in any conventional sense. It was construction: the deliberate, painstaking work of building a landscape that had never previously existed.

The Lusatian Lakeland, now Europe’s largest artificial water landscape, reaches a new milestone this month when Lake Sedlitz, the final piece of a 23-lake complex, opens for swimming and boating. The total water surface will eventually reach 144 square kilometers (56 square miles), almost exactly the size of Italy’s Lake Como.

Dr. Uwe Steinhuber of the Lausitz and Central-German Mining Administration Company (LMBV), which has overseen the project since the early 1990s, frames the scale plainly: “This is a process that will take two generations.”

Building a lake from scratch

Left alone, the craters would fill naturally over 80 to 100 years through groundwater and rainfall. The LMBV chose not to wait. Water has been extracted from three rivers, the Neisse, Spree, and Schwarze Elster, and channeled directly into the former mines.

The process requires careful coordination. Flooding can only proceed when conditions allow: power stations, shipping routes, and the fishing industry must not be disrupted. Each lake presents its own engineering problems. Embankments need geotechnical stabilisation, mineral-laden groundwater has to be managed, and in some cases, the rapid introduction of neutral river water is essential to prevent acidic runoff from reaching the lakes.

The cost has been substantial. Lusatia’s reorganisation has run to around 7 billion euros (approximately $7.6 billion). Total costs across all LMBV projects reach roughly 13.8 billion euros (about $15 billion), and a further 4.8 billion euros (around $5.2 billion) will likely be needed over the next 25 years. A single stable lake costs between 200 and 600 million euros ($220 to $660 million) to create, funded 75 percent by the federal government and 25 percent by the relevant state.

More than a tourist attraction

The results have attracted visitors from across Europe. In 2025, around 800,000 overnight stays were recorded in the region, with Czech tourism growing 12.7 percent year over year. The tourism association is now targeting the Polish market, with a long-term goal of 1.5 million annual overnight stays.

But the lakes serve a second, less obvious function. They have become water reservoirs for the Spree and Schwarze Elster rivers during drought periods, a role that grows more important as the region faces hotter, drier summers. The complex, which will eventually be linked by a navigable canal network spanning 7,000 hectares (about 27 square miles), is climate infrastructure as much as a recreation destination.

The economic shift has been felt in the communities that built their lives around coal. “The local population benefits in many ways,” says Winkler of the regional tourism association, pointing to new jobs in hospitality, leisure, and tourism infrastructure, including for former miners and their families. The region even has a town called Neu-Seeland, which translates as New Zealand, around which the artificial water landscape has grown.

A blueprint that other coal regions are watching

Winkler believes the project offers a transferable template. “The combination of comprehensive mining restoration, sustainable landscape design and the targeted development of a tourism value-added cycle provides impetus for regions facing similar structural change,” he says. International workshops began during the International Building Exhibition between 2000 and 2010, and exchanges with partners from other coal-dependent countries have continued since.

Those conversations are likely to intensify. Lausitz Energie Bergbau AG, which still operates active open-cast mines in Lusatia, plans to wind them down from 2030, with the last expected to close by 2038. Those pits will then need flooding too, extending the transformation for decades more.

Across Europe, dozens of coal regions face the same question: what do you do with the land after extraction ends? Germany has been running this experiment longer than almost anywhere else. Lusatia’s answer is that it can be built with intention, lake by lake, over two generations, into something that draws people in.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article describes a large-scale, deliberate environmental restoration and transformation project converting former coal mines into Europe's largest artificial lake district. This project has broad positive impacts including ecological restoration, climate resilience by serving as water reservoirs during droughts, economic revitalization through tourism and new jobs, and serves as a replicable model for other coal regions facing structural change. The article provides detailed context on the engineering, costs, and social benefits, making it a substantive and inspiring environmental success story.
articles.personality_title: Germany transforms coal mines into Europe’s largest lake district
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In eastern Germany’s Lusatia region, large open-cast coal mines were closed, leaving deep craters in the land. Instead of leaving these pits empty, a project began to turn them into lakes.

**What happened** – Since the 1990s, the Lausitz and Central-German Mining Administration Company (LMBV) has been filling these mine craters with water from nearby rivers. This created the Lusatian Lakeland, Europe’s largest artificial lake area, made up of 23 lakes covering 144 square kilometers, about the size of Italy’s Lake Como. The final lake, Lake Sedlitz, recently opened for swimming and boating.

**Impact** – This project changed a damaged mining region into a place for nature, recreation, and climate support. The lakes help store water during dry summers, protecting local rivers. They also attract tourists, boosting the economy and creating new jobs, including for former miners. The area around these lakes is growing, with towns like Neu-Seeland becoming popular destinations.

**What’s next step** – The remaining active mines in Lusatia will close by 2038 and will be flooded to extend the lake district. This transformation will take two generations and serves as an example for other coal regions in Europe looking to restore their land.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Germany’s Lusatia region is turning former coal mines into a large lake district that supports nature, climate, and local communities through careful planning and long-term work.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'Allemagne transforme ses mines de charbon en le plus grand district lacustre d'Europe
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans la région de Lusace, à l'est de l'Allemagne, de grandes mines à ciel ouvert ont été fermées, laissant derrière elles des cratères profonds. Au lieu de les abandonner, un projet a été lancé pour transformer ces trous en lacs.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Depuis les années 1990, la société Lausitz et Centrale Allemande d'Administration Minière (LMBV) remplit ces cratères avec de l'eau provenant des rivières voisines. Cela a créé le Lakeland de Lusace, la plus grande zone lacustre artificielle d'Europe, composée de 23 lacs couvrant 144 kilomètres carrés, une surface proche du lac de Côme en Italie. Le dernier lac, le lac Sedlitz, vient d'ouvrir pour la baignade et la navigation.

**Impact** – Ce projet a transformé une région minière endommagée en un espace naturel, récréatif et utile au climat. Les lacs stockent l'eau pendant les étés secs, protégeant ainsi les rivières locales. Ils attirent aussi les touristes, dynamisant l'économie et créant de nouveaux emplois, notamment pour les anciens mineurs. La région autour des lacs se développe, avec des villes comme Neu-Seeland qui deviennent des destinations populaires.

**Prochaine étape** – Les dernières mines actives de Lusace fermeront d'ici 2038 et seront inondées pour agrandir le district lacustre. Cette transformation prendra deux générations et sert d'exemple pour d'autres régions minières en Europe cherchant à restaurer leurs terres.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La région de Lusace en Allemagne transforme ses anciennes mines de charbon en un grand district lacustre qui soutient la nature, le climat et les communautés locales grâce à une planification soignée et un travail à long terme.
articles.personality_title_es: Alemania transforma minas de carbón en el mayor distrito lacustre de Europa
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En la región de Lusacia, al este de Alemania, se cerraron grandes minas a cielo abierto, dejando cráteres profundos. En lugar de dejarlos vacíos, se inició un proyecto para convertirlos en lagos.

**Qué pasó** – Desde la década de 1990, la compañía de administración minera Lausitz y Central de Alemania (LMBV) ha estado llenando estos cráteres con agua de ríos cercanos. Esto creó el Lakeland de Lusacia, la mayor zona de lagos artificiales en Europa, con 23 lagos que cubren 144 kilómetros cuadrados, similar al tamaño del lago de Como en Italia. El lago final, Sedlitz, se abrió recientemente para nadar y navegar.

**Impacto** – Este proyecto transformó una región minera dañada en un espacio para la naturaleza, el ocio y el clima. Los lagos ayudan a almacenar agua durante los veranos secos, protegiendo los ríos locales. También atraen turistas, impulsando la economía y creando nuevos empleos, incluso para exmineros. La zona alrededor de los lagos está creciendo, con pueblos como Neu-Seeland que se vuelven destinos populares.

**Próximo paso** – Las minas activas restantes en Lusacia cerrarán para 2038 y serán inundadas para ampliar el distrito lacustre. Esta transformación llevará dos generaciones y sirve como ejemplo para otras regiones mineras de Europa que buscan restaurar sus tierras.

**Resumen en una frase** – La región de Lusacia en Alemania está convirtiendo antiguas minas de carbón en un gran distrito lacustre que apoya la naturaleza, el clima y las comunidades locales mediante una planificación cuidadosa y trabajo a largo plazo.
articles.image_prompt: A serene, expansive landscape of deep blue artificial lakes nestled in gently rolling former coal mine craters, connected by calm waterways with small boats and swimmers, surrounded by soft earthy embankments and subtle signs of careful human engineering, under a warm, golden sky symbolizing renewal and transformation over generations.

articles.title: Why millions of adorable bees are emerging from this cemetery
articles.content:
A miner haunts the East Lawn Cemetery in Ithaca, New York. It’s not the spirit of an interred workman, but Andrena regularis, also known as the regular miner bee. It’s black and tan and fuzzy, sometimes sporting patches of yellow as it collects pollen. The critter is at once peculiar to humans and highly regular in the natural world: We might expect it to form huge colonies like honey bees, but in fact it’s among the 90 percent of bee species that are solitary. Instead of building bustling nests in trees, it digs tunnels into the ground, hence the moniker.

Scientists at nearby Cornell University have discovered that this seemingly sterilized habitat — lots of tombstones and cropped lawn — doesn’t just support this wonderful insect. It hosts one of the biggest and oldest known communities of ground-nesting bees anywhere in the world.

Great for the miner bee, to be sure. But the findings also add to a growing body of evidence that cemeteries, of all places, provide essential habitats for all kinds of wildlife, from insects to mammals. Bees are already under significant threat due to habitat loss and insecticide use, so thoughtfully managing these final resting places can protect the pollinators we need to fertilize crops amid rising temperatures and increasingly chaotic weather. “It’s exciting to see that things like this are being discovered, where you find biodiversity in unexpected places,” said Christopher Grinter, collection manager of entomology at the California Academy of Sciences, who wasn’t involved in the research. “It’s kind of this key, or this ‘aha,’ moment, where it’s like: ‘Wait, not only is this happening without us noticing, we should now encourage and foster this biodiversity.’”

Unlike social bees that amass in large nests, the regular miner bee is a solitary species that digs tunnels underground. Courtesy Bryan N. Danforth

It’s not your fault, but you might have the wrong idea about bees. We’re taught that bees live in colonies with a queen and lots of workers that produce honey. These are such essential flower-visiting pollinators that farmers rent hives to work their crops.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

As honey bees swarm farms, though, their less visible colleagues are also hard at work. The vast majority of them are solitary, making their homes underground or in natural cavities like trees. The regular miner bee, for instance, digs cavities under the East Lawn Cemetery, where it lays eggs that hatch into larvae and emerge as adults the following spring. Those adults go on to become critical pollinators for local plants, including New York’s apple trees, a highly valuable crop.

Weirdly enough, a cemetery might tick many of the boxes for a ground-dwelling buzzer in the market for a home. If this is a good spot for humans to bury their dead, it’s also a good spot for the regular miner bee: “places that don’t flood, and places that are easy to dig and don’t collapse when you dig them,” said Jordan Kueneman, a community ecologist at Cornell University and co-author of a new paper describing the findings. “So we think the bees in this area are drawn towards some of those same characteristics.”

The East Lawn Cemetery is a seemingly sterilized landscape that in fact teems with life. Courtesy Bryan N. Danforth

But if a lawn mower grazed your house, wouldn’t you think about moving? Well, this might not be too big of a deal for the regular miner bee. In fact, by cutting the grass close, groundskeepers could be doing the insects a favor. “They do like to often have the ground exposed,” Kueneman said. “That helps the ground warm up quicker, allows them to become more active more quickly in the day. It allows them to get in and out of their nests easily.”

The researchers discovered that this population of miner bees is absolutely booming. By collecting individuals and scaling that count up across the grounds, they estimate that the East Lawn Cemetery hosts between 3 million and 8 million bees, including species other than the miner. “It was an extraordinary size, and a lot of that has to do with extraordinary density,” Kueneman said. “In some locations, we were measuring thousands of individuals emerging in a square meter.” (Still, Kueneman added, gardening crews could help the bees out even more by mowing earlier in the morning, before the insects emerge for the day.)

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

The researchers could also determine that this is a healthy population because of how many females were flying around. Male regular miner bees are smaller than they are, so when a mother lays eggs, she has to put fewer resources into making male offspring. If a population has a healthy proportion of females, then, it suggests that it’s thriving, and indeed that’s what the scientists found in the cemetery.

Enter the miner bee’s mortal enemy, Nomada imbricata, a variety of cuckoo bee. Just as cuckoo birds lay their eggs in other species’ nests, this opportunist invades the miner bee’s burrows and lays its eggs. This saves it the trouble of digging its own home, and its offspring hatch with plenty of food. “The parasitic bee develops and often has these large mandibles that they use to devour everything in their path, including the host bee,” Kueneman said. “They’ll sometimes decapitate them.” Not great for the miner bee, obviously, but the cuckoo’s presence at the cemetery provides more evidence that it has a healthy population to parasitize.

The bees are not alone in their success in this unlikely habitat. Other scientists are finding that many species across the tree of life — bats, migrating geese, owls, coyotes, rare types of plants — are using cemeteries as refuges in an increasingly urbanized world. “It has a lot of the things you want,” said Seth Magle, senior director of the Urban Wildlife Institute at the Lincoln Park Zoo in Chicago, who wasn’t involved in the new research. “It’s got trees, it’s got grass, it’s potentially got prey species for you, and resources. And then it largely lacks a couple of things you don’t like about parks, which are probably people and dogs.” Also absent from cemeteries are speeding cars, which in the United States hit hundreds of millions of birds and large animals, not to mention untold numbers of insects, each year.

Wildlife cameras in cemeteries, like here in Illinois, have captured mammals and birds looking for food. Courtesy Lincoln Park Zoo

While cemeteries already shelter hoards of regular miner bees and other species, groundskeepers can do even more to support wildlife. Reducing the use of rodenticides protects birds of prey, which die when they consume poisoned rats and mice. Adding native plants provides food and shelter for native pollinators, which go on to help humans adapt to a changing climate. These species fertilize greenery across a city, for instance, significantly reducing urban temperatures, and help farmers to propagate their crops. “In order to have flowers, in order to have a beautiful ecosystem, or any biodiversity, we have to have pollinators that are fueling the reproduction of those plants,” Grinter said.

While cities have been historically cast as destroyers of biodiversity, conservationists now take a more nuanced view, Magle said. Yes, clearing forests to build metropolises is terrible for nature. But there are also ways to foster the natural world deep within cities. As places for the dead, ironically enough, cemeteries can teem with the living. “What would it look like to create a world where we continue to urbanize,” Magle said, “but we do it in a way that leaves the space for some of these species?”
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant ecological discovery that cemeteries provide essential habitats for large populations of solitary ground-nesting bees, which are critical pollinators. This finding highlights an unexpected urban biodiversity refuge with broad environmental implications, supporting pollinator conservation and urban wildlife management. The story is focused, detailed, and reveals a positive real-world impact for ecosystem health and climate resilience.
articles.personality_title: Millions of miner bees thrive in an unexpected cemetery habitat
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Bees are important pollinators that help plants grow and produce fruits. Unlike the well-known honey bees that live in large colonies, many bee species live alone and nest underground. These solitary bees are harder to spot and less understood.

**What happened** – Scientists from Cornell University studied the East Lawn Cemetery in Ithaca, New York, and found it is home to millions of regular miner bees (Andrena regularis). These bees dig tunnels in the ground to live and lay their eggs. The cemetery’s dry, easy-to-dig soil and open spaces make it a perfect place for the bees. Researchers estimated between 3 million and 8 million bees live there, with thousands emerging in just one square meter.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that cemeteries, often seen as empty or lifeless, can actually support large and healthy populations of important pollinators. Since bees face threats like habitat loss and pesticides, finding safe places for them in cities is valuable. The miner bees help pollinate local plants, including New York’s apple trees, which are important for food production. This also proves that urban areas can provide surprising homes for wildlife.

**What's next step** – Researchers suggest that cemetery managers can help bees even more by mowing grass early in the morning before bees become active and by planting native flowers to provide food. Reducing harmful chemicals like rodenticides will also protect other animals that live there. These actions can turn cemeteries into stronger refuges for many species.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Cemeteries like East Lawn provide a rare and thriving habitat for millions of solitary miner bees, showing how urban spaces can support important pollinators and wildlife.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des millions d’abeilles terricoles prospèrent dans un cimetière inattendu
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les abeilles sont des pollinisateurs essentiels qui aident les plantes à pousser et à produire des fruits. Contrairement aux célèbres abeilles domestiques qui vivent en colonies, beaucoup d’espèces d’abeilles vivent seules et nichent sous terre. Ces abeilles solitaires sont plus difficiles à observer et moins connues.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques de l’Université Cornell ont étudié le cimetière East Lawn à Ithaca, New York, et ont découvert qu’il abrite des millions d’abeilles terricoles régulières (Andrena regularis). Ces abeilles creusent des tunnels dans le sol pour vivre et pondre leurs œufs. Le sol sec et facile à creuser du cimetière, ainsi que ses espaces ouverts, en font un lieu idéal pour elles. Les chercheurs estiment que 3 à 8 millions d’abeilles y vivent, avec des milliers qui émergent au sein d’un mètre carré.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que les cimetières, souvent perçus comme des lieux vides ou sans vie, peuvent en réalité soutenir de grandes populations saines de pollinisateurs importants. Les abeilles étant menacées par la perte d’habitat et les pesticides, trouver des refuges sûrs en ville est précieux. Les abeilles terricoles contribuent à la pollinisation des plantes locales, notamment des pommiers de New York, importants pour l’alimentation. Cela prouve aussi que les zones urbaines peuvent offrir des habitats surprenants pour la faune.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs suggèrent que les gestionnaires de cimetières peuvent encore mieux aider les abeilles en tondant l’herbe tôt le matin avant l’activité des abeilles et en plantant des fleurs indigènes pour leur nourriture. Réduire l’usage de produits chimiques nocifs comme les rodenticides protégera aussi d’autres animaux. Ces actions renforceront les cimetières comme refuges pour de nombreuses espèces.

**Une phrase clé** – Des cimetières comme East Lawn offrent un habitat rare et prospère à des millions d’abeilles solitaires, montrant comment les espaces urbains peuvent soutenir des pollinisateurs et la faune essentiels.
articles.personality_title_es: Millones de abejas excavadoras prosperan en un cementerio inesperado
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las abejas son polinizadoras importantes que ayudan a las plantas a crecer y producir frutos. A diferencia de las conocidas abejas melíferas que viven en colonias grandes, muchas especies de abejas viven solas y anidan bajo tierra. Estas abejas solitarias son más difíciles de ver y menos conocidas.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos de la Universidad de Cornell estudiaron el cementerio East Lawn en Ithaca, Nueva York, y encontraron que alberga millones de abejas excavadoras regulares (Andrena regularis). Estas abejas cavan túneles en el suelo para vivir y poner sus huevos. El suelo seco y fácil de excavar del cementerio, junto con sus espacios abiertos, lo hacen un lugar ideal para ellas. Los investigadores estiman que entre 3 y 8 millones de abejas viven allí, con miles emergiendo en solo un metro cuadrado.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que los cementerios, a menudo vistos como lugares vacíos o sin vida, pueden en realidad sostener grandes y saludables poblaciones de polinizadores importantes. Dado que las abejas enfrentan amenazas como la pérdida de hábitat y los pesticidas, encontrar refugios seguros en las ciudades es valioso. Las abejas excavadoras ayudan a polinizar plantas locales, incluyendo los manzanos de Nueva York, importantes para la producción de alimentos. Esto también demuestra que las áreas urbanas pueden ofrecer hogares sorprendentes para la vida silvestre.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores sugieren que los encargados del cementerio pueden ayudar más a las abejas cortando el césped temprano en la mañana antes de que las abejas estén activas y plantando flores nativas para proporcionar alimento. Reducir el uso de químicos dañinos como rodenticidas también protegerá a otros animales. Estas acciones pueden convertir los cementerios en refugios más fuertes para muchas especies.

**Una frase clave** – Cementerios como East Lawn brindan un hábitat raro y próspero para millones de abejas excavadoras solitarias, mostrando cómo los espacios urbanos pueden apoyar polinizadores y vida silvestre importantes.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a peaceful cemetery landscape with numerous black-and-tan fuzzy miner bees gently emerging from small tunnels in the soft earth between weathered tombstones, bathed in soft natural light with muted greens and earth tones, capturing the quiet harmony of life thriving beneath a serene resting place.

articles.title: This Earth Day, meet some of the Caribbean’s national birds
articles.summary: “Birds are among the most visible symbols of our natural heritage, inspiring national pride and reminding us that our islands are home to extraordinary biodiversity found nowhere else on earth.”
articles.content:
At Global Voices, we’re often amazed at how seemingly random things remind us of how interconnected we are. Last year, it was the quality and symbolism of light; this year, after our Venezuelan contributor Estefanía Salazar shared a photo of the country's stunning national bird — the Venezuelan Troupial (Icterus icterus) — much to the delight of the rest of us in the region, it’s birds.

Not only do our feathered friends travel long distances with no consideration for the risks posed by man-made borders, but they also share our spaces, contribute to the well-being of our environment — even as their habitats are threatened by development, hunting and more — and, because they move so effortlessly between earth and sky, are powerful symbols of freedom and transformation.

According to the president of BirdLife Jamaica, Justin Saunders, “Birds, and especially our national birds, represent the resilience, pride, and significance of our countries and of the Caribbean region as a whole. Though we are small island nations, we continue to have an outsized impact, and birds reflect that strength and identity in a powerful way. They also remind us of how interconnected we are across the region […] Birds are one of the few parts of nature that are truly accessible to almost everyone, regardless of age, background, or even disability, and that unifying quality is part of what makes them so meaningful to the people of [the] Caribbean.”

This Earth Day, we offer you a glimpse at some of the region’s favourites…

Antigua and Barbuda’s magnificence

The national bird of these small islands is the Magnificent frigatebird; in fact, Barbuda is home to the largest frigatebird colony in the western hemisphere.

Locally, they are called “Weather birds” — whenever they circle inland, this behaviour is often interpreted as a reliable warning that a storm is approaching. Excellent hunters, fishermen often observe them to locate the best fishing grounds.

Posing prettily in The Bahamas

A perfect match for its blue waters and white sand beaches, the national bird of The Bahamas is the Caribbean flamingo (Phoenicopterus ruber), and its Inagua National Park, established in 1965, boasts the largest breeding site of Caribbean flamingos in the world — about 70,000 — quite an impressive figure, considering the species was once considered in danger of extinction.

The popular pelican

Various regional nations have adopted the Brown pelican (Pelicanus occidentalis) as their national bird, including Barbados, St. Kitts and Nevis, the Turks and Caicos, and St. Maarten, where it adorns the respective countries’ coat of arms. The bird can also be seen perched atop the crest of the University of the West Indies.

Up until the 1950s, there was a small island off Barbados’ west coast, aptly named “Pelican Island” after the brown pelicans that nested there in large numbers. However, the waters between Barbados and Pelican Island were reclaimed to build the Bridgetown Deep Water Harbour, and today, you only get rare glimpses of them. In St. Maarten, meanwhile, the main pelican nesting site at Fort Amsterdam has been showing declining numbers.

Phenomenal parrots

Dominica, widely known as “The Nature Island,” has a national bird found nowhere else. Known as the Imperial amazon (Amazona imperialis) or Dominican amazon parrot, fondly called the sisserou by locals, it is unfortunately a critically endangered species, with a current population trend of just 40-60 mature parrots in the wild. Their numbers have been declining due to threats such as habitat loss, climate change, and hunting.

St. Lucia also has a parrot as its national bird — its namesake, the Saint Lucia amazon (Amazona versicolor). Endemic to the island, it is considered a vulnerable species. Locals call it the Jacquot and, as one birdwatching blog recalls, the bird was in rapid decline in the 1970s because of hunting, until local environmentalists launched an awareness campaign and started going into schools dressed as parrots: “Their aim was to stress to pupils that the bird should be honoured, not hunted.” The following year, the country’s parliament passed a law making it illegal to kill the St. Lucia parrot. Such an offence is now considered unthinkable and comes with a hefty fine and jail time.

St. Vincent and the Grenadines’ parrot of choice is the indigenous Amazona guildingii, or St. Vincent parrot, which struggled in the wake of the 2021 Soufrière volcano eruption. It is currently listed as vulnerable, though the date of that assessment preceded the series of volcanic eruptions. The bird, locally called “Vincie,” is regarded as a national treasure.

More endemic species

Grenada is another regional territory whose national bird, the Grenada dove (Leptotila wellsi), is native to the island where it lives. Sadly, it is also listed as critically endangered. The most recent assessment in 2021 determined the presence of just 136–182 mature birds in the wild. Its numbers are being compromised by development and the negative impacts of the climate crisis, including pollution and invasive species. Many Grenadians have been trying to save the dove, citing its ecological significance and its role in heritage and national pride.

Haiti also honours a species endemic to the island of Hispaniola — the Hispaniolan trogon (Priotelus roseigaster) — as its national bird, underscoring the unique biodiversity of the species and the need to preserve it. Despite threats to its habitat, the Kanson wouj (“Red underpants”), as it is called in Haitian Kreyòl, is currently a species of least concern on the International Union for Conservation of Nature’s (IUCN) Red List of Threatened Species, contributing to Haitians’ perception of the trogon as a symbol of resilience. The bird is often depicted in Haitian art and folklore.

Jamaica’s ‘Doctor Bird’

When Jamaica gained its independence from Britain in 1962, the country’s National Birds Act declared the Red-billed streamertail (Trochilus polytmus) — the most common of Jamaica’s hummingbird species — as the national bird, thanks to both its endemic status and its popularity. Jamaicans fondly refer to this hummingbird as “Doctor Bird.” It has been the topic of many a Jamaican folklore tale and folk song, and forms a significant part of the island’s cultural identity.

Twin islands, two birds

At the southernmost end of the Caribbean archipelago, Trinidad and Tobago may be one nation, but it has two national birds — Trinidad’s Scarlet ibis (Eudocimus ruber) and Tobago’s Rufous-vented chachalaca (Ortalis ruficauda), commonly called the Cocrico, after the sound of its call. Both species enjoy a current status of least concern.

As BirdCaribbean Executive Director Lisa Sorenson told Global Voices via WhatsApp, “Caribbean birds, especially our endemic species, connect people to place, culture, and nature. The Caribbean is home to 185 endemic bird species; by protecting [them] and their habitats, we safeguard the health and well-being of our communities and the ecosystems we all depend on.

Birds are among the most visible symbols of our natural heritage, inspiring national pride and reminding us that our islands are home to extraordinary biodiversity found nowhere else on earth. Our islands also provide essential habitat for migratory birds, offering food, water, and shelter during their long journeys between breeding and wintering grounds.

On Earth Day, we celebrate the beauty and diversity of the natural world — and the joy, wonder, and peace that birds bring to our lives every day.”
articles.reasoning: The article highlights the significance of Caribbean national birds as symbols of biodiversity, cultural identity, and environmental resilience. It discusses conservation efforts, endangered species, and the ecological importance of these birds, which has broad environmental and societal impact. The story is focused, detailed, and celebrates biodiversity and conservation on Earth Day, inspiring hope and awareness.
articles.personality_title: Caribbean national birds showcase unique wildlife and culture this Earth Day
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The Caribbean islands are home to many special birds that represent each country's natural beauty and cultural pride. These birds face challenges like habitat loss and climate change but remain important symbols for their people.

**What happened** – For Earth Day, several Caribbean countries shared stories about their national birds. These include the Magnificent frigatebird in Antigua and Barbuda, the Caribbean flamingo in The Bahamas, the Brown pelican in Barbados and other islands, and rare parrots like the Imperial amazon in Dominica. Each bird has a unique story, with some species endangered and others thriving thanks to conservation efforts.

**Impact** – These birds help connect people to their environment and culture. For example, the Saint Lucia amazon parrot was protected after a campaign that taught children to value it. The birds also play roles in local traditions and even help fishermen find good spots. Protecting them helps keep the islands’ nature healthy and reminds everyone how closely connected the islands are.

**What's next step** – Conservation groups and communities will continue working to protect these birds and their habitats. Education and laws, like those stopping hunting of endangered parrots, show how people can make a difference. Continued efforts will be needed to face threats from development and climate change.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Caribbean national birds are living symbols of the region’s unique nature and culture, inspiring ongoing efforts to protect their future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les oiseaux nationaux des Caraïbes célèbrent la faune et la culture uniques en ce Jour de la Terre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les îles des Caraïbes abritent de nombreux oiseaux spéciaux qui représentent la beauté naturelle et la fierté culturelle de chaque pays. Ces oiseaux font face à des défis comme la perte d’habitat et le changement climatique, mais restent des symboles importants pour leurs habitants.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Pour le Jour de la Terre, plusieurs pays caribéens ont partagé des histoires sur leurs oiseaux nationaux. Parmi eux, le Magnificent frigatebird à Antigua-et-Barbuda, le flamant des Caraïbes aux Bahamas, le pélican brun à la Barbade et d’autres îles, ainsi que des perroquets rares comme l’Amazonie impériale à la Dominique. Chaque oiseau a une histoire unique, certains étant en danger et d’autres prospérant grâce à des efforts de conservation.

**Impact** – Ces oiseaux aident à relier les gens à leur environnement et à leur culture. Par exemple, le perroquet amazonien de Sainte-Lucie a été protégé après une campagne éducative auprès des enfants. Les oiseaux jouent aussi un rôle dans les traditions locales et aident parfois les pêcheurs. Les protéger contribue à garder la nature des îles en bonne santé et rappelle à tous combien les îles sont liées entre elles.

**Prochaine étape** – Les groupes de conservation et les communautés continueront à protéger ces oiseaux et leurs habitats. L’éducation et les lois, comme celles interdisant la chasse des perroquets en danger, montrent comment les gens peuvent agir. Il faudra poursuivre ces efforts face aux menaces du développement et du changement climatique.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les oiseaux nationaux des Caraïbes sont des symboles vivants de la nature et de la culture uniques de la région, inspirant des efforts continus pour protéger leur avenir.
articles.personality_title_es: Las aves nacionales del Caribe muestran la fauna y cultura únicas este Día de la Tierra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las islas del Caribe albergan muchas aves especiales que representan la belleza natural y el orgullo cultural de cada país. Estas aves enfrentan desafíos como la pérdida de hábitat y el cambio climático, pero siguen siendo símbolos importantes para sus habitantes.

Qué pasó** – Para el Día de la Tierra, varios países caribeños compartieron historias sobre sus aves nacionales. Entre ellas están el Magnificent frigatebird en Antigua y Barbuda, el flamenco del Caribe en Las Bahamas, el pelícano pardo en Barbados y otras islas, y loros raros como el amazonas imperial en Dominica. Cada ave tiene una historia única, algunas están en peligro y otras prosperan gracias a esfuerzos de conservación.

**Impacto** – Estas aves ayudan a conectar a las personas con su entorno y cultura. Por ejemplo, el loro amazonas de Santa Lucía fue protegido tras una campaña que enseñó a los niños a valorarlo. Las aves también juegan roles en tradiciones locales y ayudan a pescadores. Protegerlas contribuye a mantener la naturaleza de las islas saludable y recuerda a todos cuán conectadas están.

**Próximo paso** – Los grupos de conservación y las comunidades continuarán trabajando para proteger estas aves y sus hábitats. La educación y las leyes, como las que prohíben la caza de loros en peligro, muestran cómo la gente puede hacer la diferencia. Se necesitarán esfuerzos continuos para enfrentar amenazas por desarrollo y cambio climático.

**Resumen en una frase** – Las aves nacionales del Caribe son símbolos vivos de la naturaleza y cultura únicas de la región, inspirando esfuerzos continuos para proteger su futuro.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant Caribbean island landscape featuring a colorful assembly of the region’s national birds—Magnificent frigatebird soaring above a coastal storm, a graceful Caribbean flamingo wading in turquoise waters, a stately Brown pelican perched near a harbor, and brilliantly hued endemic parrots nestled among lush island foliage—all rendered in natural, soft tones that celebrate their unique beauty and symbolize resilience, freedom, and interconnectedness across the Caribbean.
